title: être (to be) — present tense
explanationMd: **être** is the most irregular and most used verb in French: *je suis, tu es, il/elle est, nous sommes, vous êtes, ils/elles sont*. It covers identity, description, and location — French does not split 'to be' into two verbs the way Spanish or Portuguese do. What it does NOT cover: age, hunger, heat and cold, which all use **avoir**. In speech, 'je suis' compresses to something like *chuis* — you'll hear *chuis fatigué* constantly.

commonErrors.note: Age takes avoir, not être.
Each pronoun has its own irregular form — memorize all six.

title: avoir (to have) — and where French 'has' instead of 'is'
explanationMd: **avoir**: *j'ai, tu as, il/elle a, nous avons, vous avez, ils/elles ont*. Beyond possession, French uses avoir for a whole family of states where English uses 'to be': **avoir faim** (be hungry), **avoir soif** (be thirsty), **avoir froid/chaud** (be cold/hot), **avoir peur** (be afraid), **avoir raison/tort** (be right/wrong), **avoir … ans** (be … years old), **avoir besoin de** (to need), **avoir envie de** (to feel like). Translating these literally from English ('je suis faim') is the single most recognizable beginner error.

commonErrors.note: Hunger is something you HAVE in French.
Age always takes avoir.

drills.answer: On a froid. / Nous avons froid.

title: Gender and articles: le/la/les, un/une/des
explanationMd: Every French noun is masculine or feminine, and the article carries that information: **le/un** (m.), **la/une** (f.), **les/des** (plural). Before a vowel or silent h, le and la both become **l'** (l'eau, l'homme). Gender is largely arbitrary — *le problème* but *la solution* — so learn every noun WITH its article, never bare. Useful ending patterns (with exceptions): -tion/-sion/-té/-ette are usually feminine; -age/-ment/-eau are usually masculine. Unlike English, the plural article is mandatory: 'I like dogs' = *j'aime **les** chiens*.

examples.text: Une baguette et deux croissants, s'il vous plaît.
examples.translation: A baguette and two croissants, please.
The water is cold. (elision before vowel)

commonErrors.note: French never drops the article for general statements.
-ème words from Greek are masculine: le problème, le système.

title: Regular -er verbs — present tense
explanationMd: About 90% of French verbs end in **-er** and share one pattern. Drop -er, add: **-e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent** → *parler*: je parle, tu parles, il parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent. The liberating secret of spoken French: **je/tu/il/ils forms all sound identical** — parle, parles, parlent are all [paʁl]. Only -ons and -ez sound different. So in conversation you really only juggle three sounds per verb. The endings matter mostly in writing.

examples.translation: They're listening to music. (sounds like 'il écoute')

commonErrors.note: nous always takes -ons.
vous always takes -ez.

steps.note: Sounds exactly like 'il parle' — context tells you it's plural.

title: The big four irregulars: aller, faire, venir, prendre
explanationMd: Four irregular verbs carry an outsized share of daily French. **aller** (go): vais, vas, va, allons, allez, vont — also builds the future (see futur proche). **faire** (do/make): fais, fais, fait, faisons, faites, font — covers weather (il fait beau), activities (faire du sport), shopping (faire les courses). **venir** (come): viens, viens, vient, venons, venez, viennent — and *venir de + infinitive* = 'to have just' (je viens de manger). **prendre** (take): prends, prends, prend, prenons, prenez, prennent — the ordering-and-transport verb. These four must simply be memorized; they repay it within a day.

commonErrors.note: -ons belongs only to nous.
prendre doubles the n in the ils form: prennent.

title: Negation: ne … pas (and how speech drops the ne)
explanationMd: Textbook negation wraps the verb in two pieces: **ne + verb + pas** — *je ne comprends pas*. Before a vowel, ne becomes n': *je n'aime pas*. The family extends: **ne … jamais** (never), **ne … plus** (no more/anymore), **ne … rien** (nothing), **ne … personne** (nobody). Here is the crucial real-world fact: **spoken French drops the ne almost every time** — *je comprends pas, j'aime pas, c'est pas grave*. The 'pas' does the real work. Keep ne in writing and formal speech; drop it with friends and you'll sound natural rather than sloppy. After pas, un/une/des become **de**: j'ai une voiture → je n'ai pas **de** voiture.

examples.text: Il n'y a plus de pain.
Elle ne mange jamais de viande.

commonErrors.note: The verb sits between ne and pas.
Indefinite articles become de after a negation.

drills.prompt: Say 'I never take the bus' the spoken way.

title: Asking questions: three levels of formality
explanationMd: French has three ways to ask the same question, differing only in register. **1. Intonation** (spoken, most common): keep statement order, raise your pitch — *Tu viens ?* **2. Est-ce que** (neutral, always safe): bolt it onto the front — *Est-ce que tu viens ?* **3. Inversion** (formal/written): flip verb and pronoun — *Viens-tu ?* In everyday conversation, question words just sit where the answer would: *Tu habites **où** ?* *C'est **quoi** ?* *Tu pars **quand** ?* Inversion with question words (*Où habites-tu ?*) is correct but sounds formal aloud. Master intonation + est-ce que first; recognize inversion.

examples.text: Est-ce que vous avez une table pour deux ?
examples.translation: Do you have a table for two? (est-ce que)
How much is it? (question word in place)

commonErrors.wrong: Est-ce que vous parlez anglais ? (said to a close friend)
commonErrors.note: Not wrong grammatically — wrong register. Friends get tu + intonation.

commonErrors.wrong: Où tu habites ? (in a formal email)
commonErrors.note: Casual question order looks off in formal writing.

drills.answer: Est-ce que tu habites ici ? / Où est-ce que tu habites ?

drills.prompt: Ask a stranger if they speak English (formal).
drills.answer: Parlez-vous anglais ? / Est-ce que vous parlez anglais ?

title: tu vs vous — the highest-stakes choice in French
explanationMd: **vous** is both the plural 'you' and the singular polite 'you'; **tu** is singular and familiar. Getting this wrong isn't a grammar mistake, it's a social one. Default to **vous** with: strangers, anyone serving you, colleagues until invited otherwise, anyone older. Use **tu** with: children, family, friends, fellow students, and generally anyone your age in a casual setting (a party, a hobby group). The switch to tu is often made explicit — *On peut se tutoyer ?* ('Shall we use tu?') — and once made, going back to vous is nearly impossible without irony. The verbs: **tutoyer** (use tu), **vouvoyer** (use vous). When unsure, vous — nobody is ever offended by too much politeness, only amused.

examples.translation: Can you help me, ma'am? (stranger → vous)
What are you up to tonight? (friend → tu)

commonErrors.wrong: Tu peux m'aider ? (to an older shopkeeper you've never met)
commonErrors.note: Strangers get vous, even in casual settings.

commonErrors.wrong: Vous voulez une bière ? (to a friend at their own party)
commonErrors.note: Vous with a friend creates comic distance — like calling them 'sir'.

drills.prompt: Ask a waiter for the menu.
drills.answer: Vous avez la carte, s'il vous plaît ?

title: Adjective agreement and placement
explanationMd: Adjectives agree in gender and number with their noun: add **-e** for feminine (petit → petite), **-s** for plural (petits, petites). The -e often changes the sound — the silent final consonant wakes up: *grand* [gʁɑ̃] → *grande* [gʁɑ̃d], *vert* → *verte*. Placement: most adjectives FOLLOW the noun (*un vin rouge, une ville magnifique*), but a small high-frequency set precedes it — the **BAGS** group: Beauty (beau, joli), Age (jeune, vieux, nouveau), Goodness (bon, mauvais), Size (grand, petit, gros). Three of these have special forms before a masculine vowel: *bel homme, nouvel ami, vieil immeuble*.

examples.translation: A big white house. (grand before, color after)
An old Parisian building. (vieil before vowel)

commonErrors.note: Colors always follow the noun.
beau → bel before a masculine noun starting with a vowel.

drills.prompt: Make it feminine: 'un petit café' → 'une ___ maison'.

title: Possessives: mon/ma/mes and friends
explanationMd: Possessives agree with the **thing possessed**, not the owner: **mon/ma/mes** (my), **ton/ta/tes** (your, tu), **son/sa/ses** (his/her/its — same for both!), **notre/nos** (our), **votre/vos** (your, vous), **leur/leurs** (their). So *sa voiture* is 'his car' OR 'her car' — French doesn't say which; context does. One sound-driven exception: before a feminine noun starting with a vowel, use the masculine form — *mon amie* (my female friend), *son école*, because 'ma amie' won't flow.

examples.text: C'est ma sœur et mon frère.
examples.translation: This is my sister and my brother.
Her husband is nice. (son = her, because 'mari' is masculine)

examples.text: Mon amie Sophie arrive ce soir.
examples.translation: My friend Sophie arrives tonight. (mon before vowel, even feminine)

commonErrors.note: Before a vowel, feminine nouns take mon/ton/son for the sound.

commonErrors.wrong: Elle aime sa mari. (thinking sa = her)
commonErrors.note: Agreement follows 'mari' (masculine), not the owner's gender.

title: The partitive: du, de la, des — 'some'
explanationMd: For unmeasured quantities — 'some bread', 'some water' — French requires a partitive article: **du** (m.), **de la** (f.), **de l'** (before vowel), **des** (plural): *je mange du pain, elle boit de l'eau*. English can drop 'some'; French can't drop the partitive. Two rules collapse it to plain **de**: after a negation (*pas de pain*) and after quantity words (*beaucoup de, un peu de, trop de, un kilo de*). Whole-category preferences use le/la/les instead: *j'aime LE fromage* (cheese in general) vs *je mange DU fromage* (some cheese, now).

examples.text: On achète de la bière et du fromage.
Il n'y a plus de lait.
examples.translation: There's no milk left. (de after negation)

examples.text: J'aime le vin, mais je bois peu de vin.
examples.translation: I like wine, but I drink little wine. (category vs quantity)

commonErrors.note: The partitive is mandatory where English just says 'bread'.
Quantity expressions take bare de.

drills.prompt: 'I don't eat meat': Je ne mange pas ___ viande.

title: à and de + le/les: au, aux, du, des
explanationMd: The prepositions **à** (to/at) and **de** (of/from) fuse with the definite articles: à + le = **au**, à + les = **aux**, de + le = **du**, de + les = **des**. With la and l' nothing fuses: à la gare, de l'hôtel. So: *je vais **au** marché* (to the market), *il revient **du** bureau* (from the office), *le menu **aux** champignons*. These contractions are mandatory — 'à le' or 'de les' is never correct. Note that this makes 'du' and 'des' do double duty (contraction here, partitive elsewhere) — context always disambiguates.

examples.translation: Shall we go to the restaurant?

examples.text: Je rentre du travail à dix-huit heures.
examples.translation: I get back from work at six.

examples.text: La carte des vins, s'il vous plaît.

commonErrors.note: à + le always contracts to au.
de + les always contracts to des.

title: il y a — there is, there are, and 'ago'
explanationMd: **il y a** is invariable — same form for singular and plural: *il y a un problème*, *il y a des problèmes*. Negative: *il n'y a pas de…* Question: *est-ce qu'il y a…* or spoken *y a … ?* — because in fast speech the 'il' vanishes entirely: *y a du monde !* (it's crowded!). Bonus meaning: with a time expression it means 'ago' — *il y a deux ans* = two years ago. Three uses, one tiny phrase; it's everywhere.

examples.text: Il y a un bon marché près d'ici.
examples.translation: There's a good market near here.

examples.text: Il n'y a pas de wifi ici ?
J'ai visité Paris il y a trois ans.
examples.translation: I visited Paris three years ago.

commonErrors.wrong: Il y a beaucoup des gens.
commonErrors.right: Il y a beaucoup de gens.
commonErrors.note: beaucoup takes bare de.

commonErrors.wrong: Ils y ont des problèmes. (trying to make it plural)
commonErrors.note: il y a never changes form.

drills.prompt: 'There are two cafés on this street.'
drills.answer: Il y a deux cafés dans cette rue.
Il n'y a plus de pain.

title: Futur proche: aller + infinitive
explanationMd: The everyday future is built exactly like English 'going to': conjugated **aller** + infinitive — *je vais manger* (I'm going to eat), *on va voir* (we'll see). In conversation this covers nearly all future needs; the one-word futur simple (*je mangerai*) can wait until B1 for production. Negation wraps around aller only: *je ne vais pas venir*. This is the single highest-leverage structure at A1: two verbs learned, entire future unlocked.

examples.text: Je vais prendre le plat du jour.
examples.translation: I'm going to have the daily special.

examples.text: Qu'est-ce que tu vas faire ce week-end ?
examples.translation: What are you going to do this weekend?

examples.text: On ne va pas rester longtemps.
examples.translation: We're not going to stay long.

commonErrors.note: The second verb stays in the infinitive.
ne…pas wraps the conjugated verb (aller), not the infinitive.

drills.answer: On va visiter le Louvre. / Nous allons visiter le Louvre.
Je ne vais pas sortir ce soir.

title: on — the pronoun French actually uses for 'we'
explanationMd: In spoken French, **on** has almost completely replaced **nous** as the subject: *on va au ciné* (we're going to the movies), *on est arrivés* (we've arrived). Crucially, on conjugates like il/elle — third person singular — so 'we' gets the easiest verb form: *on parle*, not *nous parlons*. It also means 'people/one/they' in general statements: *en France, on dîne tard* (in France, people eat late). 'Nous' isn't wrong — it just sounds formal or written. Learn to produce 'on' and your spoken French instantly sounds less like a textbook.

examples.text: En France, on mange beaucoup de fromage.
examples.translation: In France, people eat a lot of cheese.

examples.text: Nous vous remercions de votre visite.
examples.translation: We thank you for your visit. (formal register — nous survives here)

commonErrors.note: on takes third-person-singular forms, whatever it means.

commonErrors.wrong: Nous allons au ciné, tu viens ? (among friends — stilted)
commonErrors.right: On va au ciné, tu viens ?
commonErrors.note: Not an error, a register mismatch: spoken French says on.

drills.prompt: Say 'we're eating at eight' the natural spoken way.
Convert to spoken register: 'Nous partons demain.'

title: Passé composé with avoir — the everyday past
explanationMd: The conversational past tense: **avoir (present) + past participle** — *j'ai mangé* (I ate / I've eaten). Participles: -er → **-é** (mangé), -ir → **-i** (fini), -re → **-u** (attendu), plus irregulars worth learning as vocabulary: **eu** (avoir), **été** (être), **fait** (faire), **pris** (prendre), **vu** (voir), **bu** (boire), **dit** (dire), **mis** (mettre), **lu** (lire), **écrit** (écrire). Negation sandwiches the avoir: *je **n'ai pas** mangé*. One tense covers both English simple past and present perfect — French doesn't make that distinction.

examples.text: J'ai mangé une crêpe ce midi.
examples.translation: I ate a crêpe at lunch.

examples.text: On a pris le train ensemble.
Elle n'a pas fini son café.

commonErrors.note: The participle carries the accent: -é. 'Mange' is present tense.
prendre is irregular: pris.

drills.answer: On a bu un bon vin.

title: Passé composé with être — motion and reflexive verbs
explanationMd: A closed set of verbs — mostly motion and life-change — takes **être** instead of avoir in the past: aller, venir, arriver, partir, entrer, sortir, monter, descendre, rester, tomber, naître, mourir, retourner, passer (+ their compounds), and **every reflexive verb**. With être, the participle **agrees like an adjective**: *elle est allée*, *ils sont partis*, *elles sont restées*. So a woman writes *je suis arrivée* — same sound, extra -e. Memory hook: they're the verbs you could act out moving through a doorway. Everything else takes avoir.

examples.text: Je suis allé au marché ce matin.
examples.translation: I went to the market this morning. (male speaker)

commonErrors.note: aller is an être verb.
With être, the participle agrees — feminine adds -e.

drills.prompt: avoir or être? 'They took the metro.'
drills.answer: avoir — Ils ont pris le métro.

title: Reflexive verbs: se lever, s'appeler, se souvenir
explanationMd: Daily routine runs on reflexive verbs — the pronoun changes with the subject: **je me lève, tu te lèves, il se lève, nous nous levons, vous vous levez, ils se lèvent**. Me/te/se elide before vowels: *je m'appelle, tu t'habilles*. In the past they ALL take être (with agreement): *elle s'est levée tôt*. Negation: *je ne me lève pas*. Many verbs change meaning with se: *trouver* (find) vs *se trouver* (be located), *demander* (ask) vs *se demander* (wonder). French also uses reflexives where English uses nothing: se reposer (rest), se dépêcher (hurry), se promener (stroll).

examples.text: Je me lève à sept heures.

examples.translation: Hurry up! (imperative: pronoun after, te → toi)

examples.text: On se retrouve devant le cinéma ?
examples.translation: Shall we meet in front of the cinema?

commonErrors.right: Je me lève à sept heures.
commonErrors.note: Without 'me', lever means to lift something else.
Reflexives take être; the pronoun comes before it.

drills.prompt: 'I went to bed at midnight.' (female speaker)
drills.answer: Je me suis couchée à minuit.

title: Imparfait — the background past (introduction)
explanationMd: French has two everyday past tenses with different jobs. **Passé composé** = events, things that happened once: *j'ai mangé*. **Imparfait** = background, description, habit — how things WERE: *il faisait beau* (the weather was nice), *quand j'étais petit* (when I was little), *on allait à la plage tous les étés* (we used to go every summer). Formation: take the nous form, drop -ons, add **-ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient** — the four singular/third-plural endings all sound the same [ɛ]. At A2, produce three workhorses — **c'était** (it was), **il y avait** (there was), **il faisait** (the weather was) — and recognize the rest.

examples.text: Il y avait beaucoup de monde.
examples.translation: There were a lot of people.

examples.text: Quand j'étais étudiant, j'habitais à Lyon.
examples.translation: When I was a student, I lived in Lyon.

examples.text: Il faisait froid, donc on est restés chez nous.
examples.translation: It was cold, so we stayed home. (background imparfait + event passé composé)

commonErrors.wrong: Hier, le concert a été super. (describing how it was)
commonErrors.note: Descriptions of how something was use imparfait.

commonErrors.wrong: Quand j'étais petit, je suis allé à la plage tous les étés.
commonErrors.right: Quand j'étais petit, j'allais à la plage tous les étés.
commonErrors.note: Repeated habits in the past take imparfait.

title: Object pronouns: le, la, les / lui, leur
explanationMd: Object pronouns go BEFORE the verb: *je **le** vois* (I see him/it), *tu **la** connais ?* (do you know her?). Direct objects: **me, te, le, la, nous, vous, les** (le/la elide: je l'aime). Indirect ('to' someone): **lui** (to him/her — one form for both!) and **leur** (to them): *je **lui** parle* (I'm talking to him/her). Which one? Follow the verb's preposition: *voir quelqu'un* → direct (je le vois); *parler À quelqu'un* → indirect (je lui parle). In the passé composé the pronoun goes before avoir: *je l'ai vu*. With futur proche, before the infinitive: *je vais l'appeler*.

examples.text: Tu vois le bus ? — Oui, je le vois.
examples.translation: See the bus? — Yes, I see it.

examples.text: Elle lui a donné son numéro.
Je vais les inviter à la soirée.
examples.translation: I'm going to invite them to the party.

commonErrors.note: Object pronouns precede the verb — post-verb position is English word order.
parler à → indirect pronoun lui.

drills.prompt: Replace with a pronoun: 'Je regarde le film.'

title: y and en — the little pronouns that unlock native-sounding French
explanationMd: **y** replaces a place (à + location): *Tu vas à Paris ? — J'**y** vais demain.* (I'm going THERE tomorrow.) **en** replaces de + noun — quantities and partitives: *Tu veux du café ? — Oui, j'**en** veux bien.* (I'd like SOME.) *Tu as des frères ? — J'**en** ai deux.* (I have two OF THEM.) Both sit before the verb. English lets you answer 'do you have kids?' with 'I have two'; French requires the en: *j'en ai deux* — dropping it sounds broken. These two also live inside fixed phrases you already know: *on **y** va*, *il **y** a*, *je m'**en** vais*, *ne t'**en** fais pas* (don't worry about it).

examples.text: Tu vas souvent au marché ? — J'y vais le dimanche.
examples.translation: Do you often go to the market? — I go (there) on Sundays.

examples.text: Du fromage ? Oui, j'en prends un peu.
examples.translation: Cheese? Yes, I'll have a little (of it).

examples.text: Des enfants ? On en a trois.
Tu viens de la gare ? — Oui, j'en viens.
examples.translation: Coming from the station? — Yes, I've just come from there.

commonErrors.wrong: Tu as des frères ? — Oui, j'ai deux.
commonErrors.note: A number without its noun needs en.
y goes before the verb.

drills.prompt: Answer with y: 'Tu vas au bureau demain ?'
Answer with en: 'Vous voulez du vin ?'
drills.answer: Oui, j'en veux bien. / Non merci, je n'en veux pas.

title: Comparatives and superlatives: plus, moins, aussi … que
explanationMd: Comparison is refreshingly regular: **plus … que** (more than), **moins … que** (less than), **aussi … que** (as … as): *le train est plus rapide que le bus*. Superlative adds the article: **le/la/les plus …** — *le plus beau quartier de Paris* (note **de** where English says 'in'). Two irregulars matter: bon → **meilleur** (better) — never 'plus bon' — and bien → **mieux**: *ce café est meilleur* (this coffee is better), *ça va mieux* (I'm doing better). Quantity: *plus de temps* (more time), *moins d'argent* (less money).

examples.text: Le métro est plus rapide que le bus.
examples.translation: The metro is faster than the bus.

examples.text: C'est la meilleure boulangerie du quartier.
examples.translation: It's the best bakery in the neighborhood.

examples.text: J'ai moins de temps cette semaine.
examples.translation: I have less time this week.

commonErrors.note: bon has an irregular comparative, like English good → better.
Agreement survives inside the superlative.

drills.answer: Le vin est plus cher que la bière.

drills.prompt: 'It's the prettiest street in the city.'
drills.answer: C'est la plus jolie rue de la ville.

title: ce/cette/ces — and c'est vs il est
explanationMd: Demonstratives: **ce** (m.), **cette** (f.), **cet** (m. before vowel: cet homme), **ces** (plural) — 'this/that' undistinguished; add -ci or -là only when contrasting (*ce café-ci ou ce café-là ?*). The harder half is **c'est vs il est**. Use **c'est + article/determiner + noun**: *c'est un ami, c'est ma sœur, c'est le patron*. Use **il/elle est + bare adjective or profession**: *il est sympa, elle est médecin* (no article with professions!). General judgments about things use c'est: *c'est bon, c'est cher, c'est génial*.

examples.translation: This building is old. (cet before vowel)

examples.text: C'est un collègue. Il est très sympa.
examples.translation: He's a colleague. He's really nice. (c'est + noun, il est + adjective)

commonErrors.note: Noun with article → c'est.
Professions after il/elle est take no article.

title: The imperative: giving directions, advice, and orders
explanationMd: Drop the subject pronoun, keep the verb: **Écoute !** (tu), **Écoutez !** (vous), **Allons-y !** (nous = let's). One spelling quirk: -er verbs lose the tu-form s — *tu parles* but *Parle !* (it returns before y/en: *Vas-y !* — go ahead). Negative: wrap as usual — *Ne t'inquiète pas* (don't worry). Reflexive verbs flip the pronoun after the verb and te → toi: *Assieds-toi* (sit down), *Dépêchez-vous*. Politeness note: bare imperatives are more acceptable in French service contexts than in English ('Donnez-moi un kilo de tomates' + s'il vous plaît is perfectly polite at a market), but softening with 'tu peux…/vous pouvez…' is always available.

examples.text: Tournez à droite, puis continuez tout droit.
examples.translation: Turn right, then keep going straight.

examples.text: Ne t'inquiète pas, ça va aller.

commonErrors.note: -er imperatives drop the tu-form s.
Positive imperative puts the pronoun after, as toi.

drills.prompt: Tell a group: 'take the first street on the left.'
drills.answer: Prenez la première rue à gauche.

title: Talking about time: depuis, pendant, il y a, dans
explanationMd: Four small words carve up time. **depuis** + PRESENT tense = ongoing state: *j'habite ici depuis deux ans* (I've lived here for two years — French uses present where English uses perfect!). **pendant** = a finished duration: *j'ai vécu là-bas pendant trois ans* (for three years, over). **il y a** + past = ago: *il y a deux ans* (two years ago). **dans** + future = in (from now): *dans dix minutes* (in ten minutes). The depuis+present pattern is the one worth drilling — the English-driven 'j'ai habité ici depuis 2020' is wrong unless the living is over.

examples.text: J'apprends le français depuis six mois.
examples.translation: I've been learning French for six months. (present tense!)

examples.text: On a marché pendant deux heures.
Elle est partie il y a dix minutes.
Le train part dans cinq minutes.
examples.translation: The train leaves in five minutes.

commonErrors.wrong: J'ai habité ici depuis deux ans. (still living there)
commonErrors.note: Ongoing states take the present with depuis.
'In X time from now' is dans, not en.

drills.prompt: 'I've been working here for a year.'
drills.answer: Je travaille ici depuis un an.
On a visité Paris il y a deux ans.
Le magasin ferme dans dix minutes.

title: venir de + infinitive — 'to have just'
explanationMd: The mirror image of futur proche: conjugated **venir + de + infinitive** = to have JUST done something — *je viens de manger* (I just ate), *le train vient de partir* (the train just left). Together with aller + infinitive you get a complete near-past/near-future system with only present-tense conjugation: *je viens de finir, je vais commencer* — I just finished, I'm about to start. This construction is constant in real speech and has no one-word English equivalent, which is why learners underuse it.

examples.text: On vient de finir le dîner.
Tu viens de rater le bus.

commonErrors.note: 'Just' as recency is venir de, not an adverb.
Without de it means 'I'm coming to eat'.
